story:
Two days into a human-interest assignment and coming up empty, I got my inspiration courtesy of a misfired newspaper that bounced off my head. Lifting The Chronicle from my porch, I glanced across the street just in time to see Daniel Martin cup his wife Marjorie’s fanny as they headed inside, both backsides disappearing through the doorway. She tilted her face up and smiled at him—a sweet, unselfconscious moment after fifty-something years together. I felt like a peeping tom and a reporter on a gold mine. Remind me to thank my paperboy.

Can you imagine? Half a century in, and they still have public displays of affection. My last boyfriend treated my hand like contraband in front of witnesses—gentle in private, brotherly in public. I half-expected him to yell “Slug Bug!” and punch my arm whenever a Beetle rolled by. But I digress.

I phoned Marjorie, floated an article idea called “How to Keep Love Alive,” and she invited me over for tea. Depending on what she said, I figured I might retitle it, “How Not to End Up Doing Ten to Twenty for Wrapping Your Significant Other in a Headlock.” We’ll see.

She greeted me with a smile that crinkled into a map of fine lines and ushered me into a tidy living room, lemony with polish and punctuated by a plaid blanket flung across Daniel’s recliner. From the adjoining bedroom, a weatherman promised a boatload of sunshine. Sweet.

Over peach iced tea and homemade shortbread—good enough to make the pushy Girl Scouts up the block rethink their business model—Marjorie confided that, for the most part, they were happy. Then she gave me her secret. Relationships, she said, are like the ocean. Sometimes the tide is high: you’re in sync, laughing at nothing, finishing each other’s sentences, trading those soft, knowing glances. Then the water ebbs. Tiny things chafe. Patience thins. “Those are the times,” she said, eyes darkening then sparking again, “when I’d like to hit him.” I blinked. She grinned. “And I’m sure there’ve been days he’s wanted to toss me off a cliff. But the tide always returns.”

I set down my glass to ask the obvious next question when a loud thump came from the bedroom and the weatherman’s voice cut off mid-sunbeam. Marjorie moved like an Olympian. I followed, heart in my throat.

We found Daniel, red-faced, hoisting a TV onto the bed, the stand on its side. Marjorie rushed in with a shaky “Are you all right?” He bristled. “I wasn’t trying to do anything,” he snapped. “Heard a noise, went to the window, caught my foot on the damned cord. No big deal—go eat your cookies.” I hesitated in the doorway, but his glare passed right through me.

Back in the lemon-bright living room, Marjorie dabbed at her eyes and attempted a rueful smile. “Guess the tide has ebbed again,” she said, then muttered, “I’ve always hated the sea.”

So, yes: after fifty years there are morning walks and playful pats, and there are short tempers and tangled cords. Love, it turns out, isn’t one endless high tide—it breathes, it pulls back, it returns. And that’s the real story. Also, I’m definitely changing the title.
